analysis:
- Strong group (Heuristics 2–4,7) converge on the same successful pattern: Pareto prefiltering + cheap single‑point surrogate (rect volumes) to shortlist candidates, a lazy greedy max‑heap with stamps that recomputes exact marginal hypervolume only when an item reaches the top, and a small bounded local‑swap refinement. They reuse estimates, map shortlist→original indices, and prefer pygmo for exact HV.
- Heuristic 1 is a population/metaheuristic (sparse steady‑state GA) that can explore well but pays high overhead in Python (mutation/crossover bookkeeping, many small fitness calls) and less reuse of expensive HV results.
- Weaker ones (5,6,8,9,10,11,12,13) either do full pairwise/looped dominance checks in Python, recompute HV from scratch very often, use large Monte‑Carlo boolean tensors without bounding shortlist, or implement expensive global local searches. Common inefficiencies: repeated hv recomputation inside tight loops, vstack/copy growth per iteration, rebuilding arrays for small changes, non‑vectorized dominance checks, and unbounded Monte‑Carlo memory. Good implementations avoid these by:
  - prefiltering candidates to a compact shortlist (adaptive size, e.g. c·k),
  - initializing marginals with cheap surrogates,
  - using a stamped lazy heap so exact HV is computed only when necessary,
  - caching/storing last computed contributions and timestamps,
  - limiting local swaps and testing only promising outsiders,
  - using pygmo/C++ HV or incremental HV updates for small set changes,
  - avoiding large M×N boolean matrices unless M is tightly capped and memory is available,
  - vectorizing dominance filtering and using index maps to avoid copying point arrays.
experience: Favor a lazy‑greedy core: Pareto prefilter → top‑c·k shortlist by rect volumes → stamped heap with cached marginals and only top‑of‑heap exact HV recompute → bounded local swaps. Reuse arrays, vectorize dominance checks, cap Monte‑Carlo work, and call a C HV library (pygmo) or incremental HV routines to cut Python overhead.
current_reflection: Favor a lazy‑greedy core: Pareto prefilter → top‑c·k shortlist by rect volumes → stamped heap with cached marginals and only top‑of‑heap exact HV recompute → bounded local swaps. Reuse arrays, vectorize dominance checks, cap Monte‑Carlo work, and call a C HV library (pygmo) or incremental HV routines to cut Python overhead.
good_reflections: Prefilter non‑dominated candidates and remove points dominated by the reference. Use lazy greedy with a priority queue storing marginal HV contributions and only recompute when a candidate reaches the top. Cache incremental HV or use per‑point contribution routines instead of full recompute. Combine a cheap surrogate (R2) to shortlist candidates, then refine top ones with exact HV/local swaps. Vectorize mask math and minimize Python loops.

Always prefilter (non‑dominated), compute cheap single‑point estimates, drive a lazy max‑heap of marginal gains with stamps, recompute exact marginal HV only on heap top, then apply a small bounded local swap. Cache results, avoid rebuilding arrays/objects in loops, vectorize dominated checks, and cap stochastic search effort. This combo maximizes HV per evaluation at low runtime.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: lazy‑greedy; Pareto prefilter; top‑c·k shortlist; stamped heap; cached marginals; incremental/C HV (pygmo); local swaps; vectorize.
- Advice: Prefilter non‑dominated points, compute cheap per‑point estimates (rect volume/R2) to select top c·k, run a stamped max‑heap caching marginal HVs and recompute exact HV only when a candidate reaches the top, apply small bounded local swaps, reuse arrays and vectorize dominance/mask math, cap Monte‑Carlo effort and call C/incremental HV routines.
- Avoid: Full HV recompute for every candidate, rebuilding arrays/objects in loops, unbounded stochastic search, Python-level loops for dominance/HV.
- Explanation: Focus exact costly work on a small shortlist, reuse/caching cut Python overhead, and local swaps refine solutions cheaply.
